entry.title: MAD-HTLC
entry.summary:
In the realm of game theory, the term "dominates" signifies that one strategy consistently outperforms another under any mix ratio within a given environment. This is exemplified in scenarios such as the prisoner's dilemma, where the tit-for-tat strategy is superior to always-betray, which in turn dominates the always-cooperate strategy. Importantly, this dominance implies superiority in terms of outcome and not that one strategy encompasses another.
Further discussing strategic dominance, it's noted that sometimes adhering to a basic sub-strategy rather than opting for a more complex overarching strategy yields better results. Historical observations in chess reveal that mixed teams of computers and humans, which were once dominant, now underperform compared to standalone advanced computer AIs. This shift illustrates that integrating or overriding a sub-strategy doesn’t guarantee continued dominance, as seen when human intervention in computer strategies often leads to inferior outcomes compared to the AI operating solo.
In cryptocurrency mining, myopic (short-sighted) strategies are highlighted as predominating over non-myopic approaches due to immediate profitability. Despite potential long-term benefits for all miners if non-myopic strategies were universally adopted, the presence of even a few myopically focused miners disrupts this equilibrium. These miners gain more immediate financial rewards, thereby encouraging a shift towards myopic strategies among others. Consequently, implementing non-myopic strategies becomes economically unfeasible in such competitive environments, regardless of the simplicity or low implementation cost (150 Lines of Code as mentioned) involved.
The discourse concludes with the recognition that only a miner cartel controlling more than 50% of the hash power could enforce non-myopic strategies effectively, but such a scenario would likely jeopardize the decentralized nature and security of Bitcoin, posing existential risks to its structure and integrity. This situation underscores a fundamental challenge in strategic implementations where individual incentives conflict with collective optimality, leading to predominant adoption of strategies that favor immediate personal gain over potential communal benefit.
